data.title: China Avoids Deflation In 2025 As December CPI Hits 34-Month High
data.description:
By Lynn Song, Chief Economist, Greater China

China's consumer price index inflation rose 0.8% year-on-year in December to a 34-month high, bringing the full-year CPI to 0.0%. This indicates that China avoided deflation in 2025. Inflation has shown signs of bottoming out and should pick up further in 2026.

Inflation continues to trend higher

China's consumer price inflation rose 0.8% year-on-year in December, up from 0.7%, the highest level since February 2023. The read came in line with market expectations.

As in November, the acceleration in inflation remains primarily attributable to rising food prices. Food inflation picked up to a 1.1% pace YoY, a 14-month high. Fresh vegetable and fresh fruit prices saw the largest month-on-month upticks - up 18.2% and 4.4% YoY, respectively.

Pork prices continued to fall, but saw a slightly smaller drag in year-on-year terms, rising 0.4pp to -14.6% YoY. The YoY level has risen for three consecutive months. We expect the drag from pork price deflation to turn around this year.

Non-food inflation, on the other hand, remained unchanged at 0.8% YoY. Household appliance prices rose 5.9% YoY as we began to see an impact from 2024's trade-in policy discounts feed through the economy. Services inflation, in the form of tourism (2.1%) and healthcare (2.9%), generally outpaced goods inflation.

The transportation and communication category continued to weigh on inflation, down 2.6% YoY, primarily due to declines in transportation (-1.9%), fuel (-8.2%), and communication appliances (-0.7%).

We’re also seeing the continued impact of the property market malaise, with the residence component of inflation in deflation at -0.2% YoY, driven by a -0.3% YoY drop in rents. Core inflation was unchanged for a third consecutive month at 1.2% YoY.

Producer price index inflation rose to -1.9% YoY, up from -2.2% in November. This level marked a 16-month high and the 39th consecutive month of PPI deflation.

Food prices continued to push inflation higher in December

Is the worst over for China's deflationary pressure?

After three straight years of deflationary pressures, could we finally see inflation pick up a little more convincingly in 2026?

Various factors suggest that this could be possible:

  • Non-food inflation shows a mixed picture but has generally been benign in 2H25.
  • Food prices were in deflation for 9 of 12 months in 2025, and the base effect will likely lead to a pickup in YoY levels in 2026. A turnaround of the pork cycle would further contribute to upward food prices.
  • China's anti-involution push could help alleviate downward price pressures, though it remains unclear how much of an impact we will see.
  • Potential new avenues for supporting consumption on top of trade-in policies, which, thanks to discounts, tend to exacerbate deflationary pressure.

As such, we're looking for 0.9% CPI inflation in 2026. While this remains well short of the 2% inflation target pursued by many central banks globally, it would nonetheless be a healthy development after three consecutive years of annual CPI inflation of 0.2% or lower.

Despite expectations of a recovery, inflation remains relatively low and should not preclude further monetary easing this year. We continue to expect there's a case for further rate cuts this year, with a 10 bp cut sometime in 1H26.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Top 5 Mining Stocks To Watch In 2026: No.3 - Taseko Mines
data.description:
The year 2026 is here, and it is time for the traditional "Top 5 Mining Stocks to Watch" list. 2025 was a huge success, as the record-breaking metals prices pushed the featured companies to triple-digit returns. As of December 29, Aris Mining (ARM) was up 360%, followed by Taseko Mines (TGB) (186%), and Eldorado Gold (EGO) (141%). In the case of Calibre Mining, the calculation is a little more complex, as in June, the company was acquired by Equinox Gold (EQX). However, taking into account the 2024 closing price of Calibre, and the current value of 0.31 Equinox shares, Calibre is technically nearly 102% up. The least successful pick was Franco-Nevada (FNV), although a 78% gain can hardly be taken as a failure. On average, the 2025 Top 5 list gained 191.4%, the VanEck Junior Gold Miners ETF (GDXJ) (169%), the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX) (154%), the SPDR S&P Metals and Mining ETF (XME) (86%), and also the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) (17%).

It is not realistic to expect similar gains also in 2026, as the gold, silver, and copper prices will most probably ease the pace of growth (and possibly even give up a portion of the 2025 gains), however, numerous mining companies expect some major catalysts and have a good probability of outperforming their peers. Just like every year, I know that there are definitely some other companies with high potential that could have been considered for inclusion into the Top 5. If you are missing such a company on the list, please feel free to mention it in the comments section.

The fifth place of the 2026 list captured NexGold Mining (NXGCF), and the fourth place captured U.S. Gold Corp. (USAU). No. 3 on the list belongs to Taseko Mines (TGB). Taseko was No. 1 on the 2025 list, and it did well, gaining nearly 190%. However, the numbers show that a major part of the growth can be attributed to the 40% copper price growth recorded in 2025. And positive company-specific fundamentals, such as the Florence mine that is starting production right now, and the growing value of the Yellowhead and New Prosperity projects, are yet to be reflected by Taseko's share price. Therefore, unless a copper price collapse occurs or a negative surprise emerges, Taseko should experience some major gains also in 2026.

The Current State of Taseko Mines

For years, Taseko's cornerstone asset has been the Gibraltar copper mine. It is located in British Columbia, and it should be producing around 130 million lb copper per year on average, until 2044. In 2024, Gibraltar produced 106 million lb copper and 1.4 million lb molybdenum, at a C1 cash cost of $2.66/lb copper. Over the first three quarters of 2025, it was 67.4 million lb copper and 1.07 million lb molybdenum, at a C1 cash cost of $2.75/lb copper. The 2024 and H1 2025 production volumes were lower than the expected long-term averages, as a lower-grade portion of ore was mined. However, in Q3 2025, the production exceeded 25 million lb copper, and it should further improve in the near future. Based on a recent technical study, the Gibraltar mine has an after-tax NPV (8%) of C$3 billion ($2.31 billion) at a copper price of $5/lb. At the current copper price of $5.8/lb, the number should be around C$3.9 billion ($3 billion).

But the times when Gibraltar was the only producing asset are gone now. The Florence copper mine was completed only recently. The well-field operations commenced in October, and the first copper cathode production should be announced over the coming weeks. The mine should be producing around 85 million lb copper per year, over an initial 22-year mine life. The C1 cash cost was projected only at $1.11/lb copper. The production rates should be growing over the course of 2026, and the full capacity should be reached by 2027. As can be seen, Florence will significantly boost Taseko's copper production, moreover, its unit production costs should amount to less than half of Gibraltar's production costs. At a copper price of $5/lb, the after-tax NPV (8%) of the Florence project should be around $1.6 billion. And at the current market price of $5.8/lb, it should be slightly over $2 billion.

After Florence is up and running, Taseko's main attention should shift to the Yellowhead project. It is located in British Columbia, and it contains measured and indicated resources of 7.12 billion lb copper, 1.165 million toz gold, and 50.15 million toz silver. The inferred resources contain an additional 577 million lb copper, 91,500 toz gold, and 4.2 million toz silver. What is important, a substantial portion of the resources is also included in reserves, which contain 5.04 billion lb copper, 790,000 toz gold, and 34 million toz silver. According to the July 2025 technical study, Yellowhead should be producing 178 million lb copper per year, at a C1 cash cost of $1.9/lb on average, over a 25-year mine life. The CAPEX was estimated at C$2 billion ($1.54 billion). At a base-case copper price of $4.25, the after-tax NPV (8%) should equal C$2 billion ($1.54 billion). However, it should be around C$3 billion ($2.3 billion) at a copper price of $5/lb, and slightly over C$4 billion ($3.1 billion) at the current copper price of $5.8/lb.

The New Prosperity project is one of the biggest undeveloped gold-copper deposits in the world. It contains measured and indicated resources of 13.3 million toz gold and 5.3 billion lb copper. The reserves contain 7.7 million toz gold and 3.6 billion lb copper. According to an outdated feasibility study, the mine should reach an average annual production of approximately 230,000 toz gold and 110 million lb copper. Over the first 5 years, it should be even 300,000 toz gold and 130 million lb copper on average. The project has been idled for nearly a decade, due to disputes with the First Nations and related permitting issues. However, in June 2025, an agreement between Taseko, the Tŝilhqot'in Nation, and the Province of British Columbia was reached. According to the agreement, Taseko will hold a 77.5% interest in New Prosperity, with the remainder held by the Tŝilhqot'in Nation. However, Taseko agreed "not to be the proponent (operator) of mineral exploration and development activities at New Prosperity", therefore, it will probably try to sell the property or to find a JV-partner that would serve as the operator.

There is also the Aley project. It is 100%-owned by Taseko. It lies in British Columbia, and it contains one of the biggest niobium deposits in the world. The measured and indicated resources contain 739 million kg (1.63 billion lb) of niobium, of which 293 million kg (646 million lb) are included in the reserves. Back in 2014, the initial CAPEX for the Aley mine was estimated at C$870 million ($777 million, using the September 2014 USD/CAD exchange rate of 1.12). The mine should produce 9 million kg of niobium per year, over a 24-year mine life. The production cost was estimated at $21/kg. The after-tax NPV(8%) was estimated at C$480 million. However, all the numbers are 12 years old, and they must be taken with a grain of salt.

In 2024, Taseko generated revenues of $432 million, operating cash flow of $161.8 million, and net income of -$9.4 million. Over the first three quarters of 2025, it was $306.9 million, $84.1 million, and -$23.7 million, respectively. However, as mentioned above, the numbers have been negatively affected by lower-grade ore being mined at Gibraltar in H1 2025, and mill repairments in H2 2024. The numbers should improve notably in 2026, and even more in 2027, mainly due to the Florence mine ramp-up.

The 2026 Catalysts

The first major catalyst should arrive in early 2026. The first copper production at the Florence mine should be announced over the coming weeks. Subsequently, the quarterly operating results will be closely followed. Every positive report showing steady growth of production at Florence should serve as a catalyst. If everything goes well, the full capacity should be reached by the end of 2026. Further upside may be provided by Gibraltar, if the production rates finally approach the projected long-term averages (130 million lb copper per year, or 32.5 million lb copper per quarter).

Some further progress could be reached at Yellowhead or New Prosperity. In the case of Yellowhead, the potential news should be related to the permitting process. In the case of New Prosperity, some news regarding the sale of the property, or finding a JV-partner could serve as a catalyst, especially if the negotiated terms are attractive.

But Yellowhead and New Prosperity shouldn't be the main factors in 2026. All it takes for Taseko's share price to double again, is for Gibraltar and Florence to operate at the projected long-term production rates, and ideally also at the projected production costs. Of course, assuming that the copper price remains above $5/lb. As stated above, at a copper price of $5/lb, the after-tax NPV(8%) of Gibraltar and Florence is $2.31 billion, and $1.6 billion, respectively. It means nearly $4 billion in total. And at the current copper price of $5.8/lb, it is even $5 billion in total. Taseko's market capitalisation is $2.13 billion right now. Yes, Taseko hedges a portion of its production, which reduces the average realised copper price, however, only slightly. According to the latest earnings call, Taseko had collar contracts with a floor price of $4/lb copper and a ceiling price of $5.4/lb copper, on 27 million lb copper for Q4 2025, and on 54 million lb copper for 2026. And yes, Taseko had a net debt of $560 million as of the end of Q3 (it should be below $400 million now, taking into account the $170 million bought deal financing, and cash generated in Q4). On the other hand, Yellowhead, New Prosperity, and Aley are worth at least several hundred million dollars. In other words, at the current copper price, if Gibraltar and Florence operations reach the projected numbers, Taseko should be worth well over $4 billion, which means double the current market capitalisation, easily.

Conclusion

Yes, as always, there are risks. First of all, it is the copper price. Right now, it is slightly over $5.8/lb copper. The fundamentals are positive, and it is expected that the copper market deficit is going to grow rapidly over the coming years. However, any weakness of the global economy can send the prices substantially lower, at least for some time. This would have a negative impact on Taseko's share price, regardless of the company-specific events.

There are also operational risks. The Gibraltar mine experiences some production disruptions relatively often. Moreover, although the pilot plant at Florence was successful, the risks related to the ramp-up process of the full-scale operation still exist.

In terms of Yellowhead, there is the permitting risk. Any negative news can have a negative impact on the share price.

However, the upside potential is very attractive, and it outweighs the risks. Taseko's current market capitalisation is $2.13 billion, and the enterprise value should be below $2.5 billion, possibly around $2.4 billion right now. As shown above, at the current copper price, the after-tax NPV (8%) of Gibraltar and Florence is around $5 billion. Even if the impacts of the hedging on the average realised copper price are taken into account, the value remains well above $4 billion. And some value must be attributed also to Yellowhead, New Prosperity, and Aley. In other words, if the copper price doesn't experience a major decline, Gibraltar approaches its full production capacity, and Florence ramp-up progresses well, Taseko's share price should double easily by the end of 2026.

data.title: Canadian Imperial Bank of Commerce: Shares Look Pricey On Strong Results
data.description:
Like other members of the Big Five Canadian banks, Canadian Imperial Bank of Commerce (CM) (CM:CA) ("CIBC") has been a strong performer of late. Shares of the Bay Street giant have returned over 40% since my last update in the summer, comfortably outpacing the major indices either side of the border. While much ink has been spilled on the possible threats facing CIBC's high-quality Canadian operations (U.S. tariffs, elevated house prices, deteriorating credit quality, and so on), domestic profits have continued to grow, while the firm has also been riding a very nice tailwind in its Capital Markets division. All of this has been great for CIBC's bottom line, which grew nearly 20% in its fiscal 2025. It has also been great for the firm's longstanding dividend (nearly 160 years of consecutive payments and counting), with the Board recently increasing the quarterly payout by an inflation-busting 10%.

At the risk of being a party pooper, I do have some concerns. Capital Markets income can be pretty volatile for one; and while that division has contributed nicely recently, normalizing trading income could be a near-term headwind. I would make a similar argument regarding valuation. Sentiment seems to be firmly in CIBC's favor right now, with the market rewarding its strong operating performance with a further round of re-rating. With the stock trading for over 2 times book value and yielding 3.4%, that's left CIBC looking expensive relative to historical norms. Hold.

Domestic Profits Growing Nicely

(All financial figures in Canadian dollars unless stated otherwise)

Last year may have been a volatile one for Canadian economic sentiment, but CIBC's domestic profits have continued to grow nicely. The firm booked a $4.46 billion net profit in its Canadian Personal and Commercial Banking segment last year, around 7% higher than 2024. This mapped to a strong 21.6% return on equity, 70 basis points above 2024's print.

CIBC achieved this even as it had to set aside more to cover potential bad debt. Credit charges in that segment clocked in at $1.92 billion last year, up 40% on 2024. Asset quality metrics have continued to slip since my last update, with delinquencies rising in both mortgages and credit cards. Uncertainty being very much the theme of the period, CIBC boosted its group-wide loss reserve by 15% last year.

Interest margin expansion is one good reason to be sanguine about this. Indeed, while CIBC has seen a significant rise in credit charges, higher net interest income has easily covered this. In turn, this line has benefited from a widening interest margin, with CIBC's Canadian P&CB unit reporting a 2.79% net interest margin last year. Not only was that 16 bps higher than in 2024, but its NIM has been growing sequentially, reaching 2.90% by the final quarter. Because margin-driven growth doesn't attract any incremental costs, this has been great for operating leverage. Revenue growth in Canadian P&CB outpaced cost growth by 340 basis points last year, driving roughly 13% growth in pre-provision income.

Turning back to interest margin expansion, there have been a few drivers of this. One is CIBC's so-called "tractoring" strategy, also known as structural hedging. A simple way to think about this it to imagine a laddered bond portfolio, funded by deposits. As one set of bonds matures, another is bought as a replacement. If these bonds have, say, a 5-year maturity at purchase, then the portfolio will ultimately capture the 5-year rolling-average yield. I've simplified things, but hopefully you see where this is going: the margin on these investments has been creeping up, reflecting the higher interest rate environment post-COVID.

Mix effects have also been working in CIBC's favor. Demand deposit growth has outstripped total deposit growth, and this type of funding tends to be relatively cheap. Deposits costs have also fallen on the back of lower short-end rates. Mix effects may also have contributed positively on the asset side, with growth in high-yielding credit card balances outpacing other loan types in CIBC's Canadian retail unit last year.

Buoyant financial markets have also been a tailwind. CIBC sported just $175 billion in Canadian mutual funds and ETFs at the end of its fiscal 2025. It also owns Investor's Edge, a self-directed investing platform. Rising markets have been a boon to these business, lifting assets under administration and fee income.

Thinking about the outlook, some of these trends could continue into 2026. On the credit side, initial guidance sees provisioning on impaired loans in the mid-to-low 30 basis points area. That's about the same as fiscal 2025 (33 basis points). Positively, Canadian unemployment has ticked down since the end of CIBC's fiscal year (October), which should be credit positive.

Meanwhile, management continues to be bullish on interest margins, which will benefit from easier comps early in the year. Financial markets have also continued to rise since the end of CIBC's financial year – and that'll be good for fee income. All said and done, 2026 could be shaping up to be another solid year for CIBC's Canadian operations.

Capital Markets Surge

As good as CIBC's domestic retail and commercial banking performance has been, Capital Markets has been the star of the show recently. This segment kicked in $2.27 billion in net income last year, up 40% on 2024. At 22.1%, the associated return on equity was up around 420 basis points.

This is where I have a slight concern in terms of the outlook. Trading income accounts for the majority of that segment's revenue. While this line has benefited from last year's market volatility, the ensuing normalization is going to be a headwind at some point. On the flip side, the outlook for investment banking activity is better, as lower interest rates typically helps this business.

Valuation

CIBC's valuation remains my main sticking point, though, as the stock's multiples have re-rated further since my last piece. At $126.29 in Toronto trading, CIBC is currently trading for just over 2 times its book value ($62.33 per share). This represents a circa 30% premium to its 10-year average (~1.5 times).

The dividend yield is telling a similar story. CIBC is paying a quarterly distribution of $1.07 per share, for an annualized yield of 3.4%. That's roughly 160 basis points below its 10-year average (~5.0%), while the shares were yielding over 4% when I last covered them.

At this point, I think today's buyer will need CIBC's multiples to stay elevated in order to realize a 10%-plus annualized return. Maybe that will happen, but its not an assumption I would like to make. For that reason, I'll be keeping CIBC on the watch list for now.

data.title: IJT: Why The Tide May Turn For Small-Cap Growth In 2026
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The iShares S&P Small-Cap 600 Growth ETF (IJT) gained only 5.26% last year compared to 21.94% for the iShares S&P 500 Growth ETF (IVW), marking this small-cap growth benchmark's third straight year of disappointing returns. However, there are signs that point to a possible turnaround this year. IJT's 20.59x TTM P/E represents a 40% discount to IWV, and its next-year earnings growth rate is now 17% after last month's rebalance. I'm doubtful IJT is a compelling long-term holding due to its poor quality profile, but the ETF is worth considering as a tactical overweight in 2026, so I've assigned it a "buy" rating, and I look forward to explaining why in detail below.

iShares S&P Small-Cap 600 Growth ETF Overview

Strategy Discussion and Fund Characteristics

According to its website, IJT tracks the S&P SmallCap 600 Growth Index, which is "composed of small-capitalization U.S. equities that exhibit growth characteristics," including fewer stocks with strong value characteristics. S&P Style Indexes rank stocks on separate growth and value dimensions based on the six factors listed below, per its methodology.

The Index relies on backward-looking metrics like a stock's three-year sales and earnings per share growth rate and its trailing P/B, P/E, and P/S ratios. For small-cap stocks priced primarily on future expectations, this is potentially problematic.

IJT's key fund characteristics include a 0.18% expense ratio, a 0.13% 30-day median bid/ask spread, and a 345 current holdings count. The ETF has amassed $6.46B in assets under management since its inception on July 24, 2000, and its $145.28 close price per share as of January 7, 2026, represents a 0.03% premium to its NAV.

IJT Sector Comparison vs. IJK and IVW

As of December 31, 2025, IJT's largest allocations were in the Industrials and Technology sectors at 19.77% and 19.38%, respectively. It also has high allocations to Health Care and Financials, with minimal allocations to defensive sectors like Consumer Staples and Utilities. The composition itself is growth-oriented, as many of its top sectors have earnings variability, which I'll touch on later. However, what's notable is how different IJT's composition is from IVW, which has 48.84% allocated to Technology. Much of that is concentrated in a few mega-cap names like Nvidia (NVDA) and Microsoft (MSFT), so IJT should benefit if markets broaden beyond mega-cap tech.

iShares S&P Small-Cap 600 Growth ETF Performance Analysis

IJT's returns distribution chart has been even over the last 20 years. As shown below, 22.9% of monthly returns fell in the -5% to 0% range, with another 44.2% in the 0% to 5%. That means that roughly one-third of monthly returns were outside this range, and it's the really big ones that are unpredictable and can get investors into trouble if they're not able to withstand those losses, even if they're temporary. In IJT's case, its worst month was a 21.46% loss in October 2008; IJK and IVW lost 21.49% and 16.50% that month, so as bad as the Great Financial Crisis was for large-cap growth stocks, the small- and mid-cap segments fared even worse.

IJT has also realized higher volatility relative to IWV, and it's important to state that this added volatility was not always beneficial. I calculated a 0.57 Sortino Ratio, a standard measure of downside risk-adjusted returns, for IJT, which was the lowest in the group over this period. Even though IJT delivered some substantial gains (+e.g., 87.45% in the one-year ending March 2021), it wasn't enough to overcome its 51.05% max drawdown or its -42.00% "worst 12 months" return. The result was an annualized return lag of 3.42% per year.

iShares S&P Small-Cap 600 Growth ETF Fundamental Analysis

The next table highlights selected fundamental statistics for IJT's top 20 holdings, which comprise 19.16% of the portfolio. You'll notice that several of these statistics are outliers, like Arrowhead Pharmaceutical's (ARWR) 23,258% one-year trailing sales growth rate. This isn't unusual for small-cap stocks, but please note that when calculating the summary statistics at the bottom, I've taken care to cap outliers at 50%. Therefore, I believe the estimate is reasonable and not overstated.

On a weighted average basis after adjusting for outliers, IJT's historical and estimated sales and earnings per share growth rates are in the double-digits across the board. I think they're just as good as IJK's, and although they're lower than IVW's, IJT is arguably more attractive when considering its P/E ratios. For example, on a trailing basis (3Y EPS CAGR, TTM P/E), the modified PEG ratios for each fund are as follows:

  • IJT: 20.59x / 12.86% = 1.60x
  • IJK: 27.59x / 16.18% = 1.71x
  • IVW: 34.05x / 23.88% = 1.43x

And on a forward-looking basis (1Y FWD EPS, FWD P/E):

  • IJT: 18.74x / 17.47% = 1.07x
  • IJK: 23.42x / 19.33% = 1.21x
  • IVW: 30.07x / 26.75% = 1.12x

These results show that from a GARP perspective, IJT ranks either #1 or #2, depending on which statistic you value most. Furthermore, this picture of accelerating growth is supported by IJT's components' 11.71% EPS surprise last quarter. Sales surprises were also high at 4.37%, so to me, the upside potential looks strong.

Countering this bullish outlook is the fact that IJT is a fairly low-quality fund. The fundamental analysis table highlights how its 13.62% ROE is barely one-third that of IVW's 34.18% figure, and it also trails IVW substantially on weighted average EBIT margins (15.38% vs. 35.93%) and net margins (15.38% vs. 17.26%). IJK has slightly better margins, but in general, it's difficult to get around the quality issue with this size segment. That's what makes it a questionable long-term investment.

Investment Recommendation

If your risk tolerance is high, I view IJT as a smart, small-sized tactical play for 2026. While it has persistent quality issues almost by design, its P/E ratios are low enough to mitigate significant price-multiple compression in a market downturn, while its growth rates are high enough to participate well in an upwards-trending market. I'm encouraged by its components' high sales and earnings surprises last quarter, and I think they will lead to strong gains if market sentiment can remain positive. As a result, I've assigned IJT a "buy" rating, and I look forward to answering any questions you might have in the comments section below. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Jobs: Every Jobs Report In 2025 Has Been Revised Lower
data.description:
The analysis below covers the employment picture released on the first Friday of every month. While most of the attention goes to the Headline Report, it can be helpful to look at the details, revisions, and other reports to get a better gauge of what is really going on.

Current Trends

The jobs report showed a very modest increase of 50k jobs in December. This followed an estimated loss of 173k jobs in October and only 56k jobs added in November. The Household Survey report was actually much higher at 232k jobs for the month of December.

Figure 1: Primary Report vs. Household Survey – Monthly

For the year, the Household Survey is significantly higher due to the data anomaly in January. This was a one-time event, but the Household Report has been overperforming this year.

March, April, June, August, September, November, and December all saw beats by the Household Survey. Not all of these were beats at the time, but the downward revisions of the Headline Report have been severe enough to create the underperformance.

Figure 2: Primary Report vs. Household Survey – Annual

Switching to the headline report…

The BLS publishes the data behind their birth-death assumptions (formation of new business). In December, the BLS assumed a loss of 67k jobs, which is on top of a loss of 125k for the baseline number.

Figure 3: Primary Unadjusted Report With Birth-Death Assumptions – Monthly

For the year, the birth-death assumption is a positive 1.15M against negative actuals of -633k. This means all positive growth YTD is directly tied to the birth/death assumptions.

Figure 4: Primary Unadjusted Report With Birth-Death Assumptions – Monthly

Digging Into the Headline Report

Unfortunately, despite being highly unreliable, the Headline report is the best data we have for the more recent periods. Furthermore, this is the data the Fed uses to shape its policy. The 50k jobs number was accompanied by a fall in the unemployment rate to 4.4%.

Figure 5: Change by sector

Jobs by Category

When looking at the last 12-month trend, only 3 of the categories were actually above trend, with 5 below.

Figure 6: Current vs. TTM

The table below shows a detailed breakdown of the numbers.

Figure 7: Labor Market Detail

Revisions

This is the biggest story of the jobs report. Every single month in 2025 has seen negative revisions. This destroys the credibility and integrity of the report. Several months saw massive downward revisions, such as March, May, June, August, and now November. Revisions cut the numbers in half or even brought the job growth negative.

Figure 8: Revisions

Over the last twelve months, jobs have been revised down by about 59k per month and revised lower by 80.7k over the last three months!

Figure 9: Revisions

More Detail in the Household Survey

Another level of detail in the household report shows full-time vs. part-time job holders. This month shows that full-time jobs were gained while part-time were lost.

Figure 10: Full-Time vs. Part-Time

Historical Perspective

The chart below shows data going back to 1955.

Figure 11: Historical Labor Market

The labor force participation rate is still well below the highs before the Global Financial Crisis. This month it increased slightly to 62.4%.

Figure 12: Labor Market Distribution

Conclusion

The jobs revisions have gotten so bad that even Jerome Powell has said something about it. In the report last month, the QCEW showed an even more dire picture. Not only are the reports weak, but the downward revisions show an even weaker economy combined with a report that people can no longer trust.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: The AI Revolution Showed Up In The Data: The '1940s Boom' Is Here
data.description:
We've talked about the formula for an early '40s-like boom-time economy.

We got some data this week that supports it.

As we've discussed over the past three years in these notes (since the May 2023 "Nvidia (NVDA) moment"), we are (still) in the early days of what should be the most productivity-enhancing technological advancement of our lifetime - generative AI.

On that note, Q3 productivity data was reported Thursday morning. And it was big, 4.9%. For perspective, we averaged less than 1% productivity growth for the decade prior to the pandemic.

Remember, hot productivity growth does two things: 1) creates the opportunity for the continuation of much-needed real wage growth (to restore living standards, which were eroded by inflation), and 2) increases the long-term potential growth rate of the economy.

With that, if we look at the four engines of GDP - consumption, investment, government spending, and net exports - they are all firing.

The Fed is backstopping liquidity and cutting rates (more this year), which makes borrowing cheaper, makes saving less attractive and creates the confidence necessary to promote hiring. That will keep consumption going.

We know the investment story. It's "build it here" policy incentives, which includes full expensing in year one.

The One Big Beautiful Bill is driving government spending tailwinds.

And tariff policies are aggressively narrowing the deep trade deficit - which lessens its drag on growth.

With all of this, we had a hot net exports number. And, related, we had a hot Q4 GDP estimate from the Atlanta Fed model (now running 5.4%).

This is the kind of growth you'd expect (nominal growth around 8%), or better, after blowing up the money supply - 10 years' worth of money supply growth over just a two-year period in response to the pandemic.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: U.S. IPO Weekly Recap: Aktis Oncology Leads As First Sizable 2026 IPO
data.description:
Three IPOs and five SPACs priced this week. Six IPOs and two SPACs submitted initial filings.

In the first sizable deal of 2026, solid tumor biotech Aktis Oncology (AKTS) priced its upsized IPO at the high end of the range to raise $318 million at a $1.0 billion market cap. The company is a Phase 1 biotech developing alpha-emitting radiopharmaceuticals against prevalent solid tumors. Preclinical studies suggest superior efficacy vs. Padcev, but Aktis faces risk given its early-stage pipeline and lack of human data until at least 1Q27. Its pipeline features Ac-AKY-1189 for Nectin-4-expressing cancers and Ac-AKY-2519 targeting B7-H3-expressing tumors. Aktis finished the week up 24%.

Texas-based juice manufacturer Buda Juice (BUDA) priced its IPO at the low end of the range to raise $20 million at a $95 million market cap. The company states that it is pioneering the "UltraFresh" juice category through its end-to-end cold chain platform. Its product portfolio consists of fresh citrus juices and wellness shots across multiple brand lines and retail customers, which it believes demonstrates both brand diversification and private label manufacturing capabilities. Buda Juice finished the week up 9%.

While not included in the table below, Brazilian mineral exploration company Atlas Critical Minerals (ATCX) priced its upsized IPO at the low end of the range to raise $10 million at a $40 million market cap. The company’s portfolio includes rare earth and titanium prospects in Goiás and Minas Gerais, graphite properties in Minas Gerais, copper and nickel rights in Goiás and Piauí, and uranium-bearing areas across six states in Brazil. Atlas finished the week up 10%.

Five SPACs also priced this week. Lafayette Digital Acquisition (ZKPU) raised $250 million to target financial services and tech. Bleichroeder Acquisition II (BBCQU) raised $250 million to target technology. Daniel Cohen’s Art Technology Acquisition (ARTCU) raised $220 million to target tech, art, and finance. Soren Acquisition (SORNU) also raised $220 million to target healthcare. Black Spade Acquisition (BIIIU) raised $150 million to target leisure, entertainment, and digital assets.

7 IPOs During the Week of January 5th, 2026

Blank check company formed by SPAC veteran Daniel Cohen targeting tech, art, and financial services.

Soren Acquisition (SORNU)

Blank check company targeting the healthcare sector.

Six IPOs submitted initial filings. Brazilian digital bank PicPay (PICS) filed to raise an estimated $500 million. Budget furniture retailer Bob's Discount Furniture (BOBS) filed to raise an estimated $400 million. Electrical distribution equipment manufacturer Forgent Power Solutions (FPS) filed to raise an estimated $200 million. Hair loss biotech Veradermics (MANE) filed to raise $100 million. Oncology biotech Eikon Therapeutics (EIKN) filed to raise $100 million. Drug-resistant infection treatment developer Lakewood-Amedex Biotherapeutics (LABT) filed for a direct listing on the Nasdaq.

Two SPACs also submitted initial filings. Willow Lane Acquisition II (WLIIU) plans to raise $125 million to target consumer goods, gaming, and leisure. Cantor Equity Partners VI (CEPS) plans to raise $100 million to target financial services, digital assets, and healthcare.

Operates a digital banking platform in Brazil.

Week Ahead

No IPOs are currently scheduled in the week ahead, although smaller issuers may join the calendar throughout the week.

A wealth of sizable companies on file remain eligible to launch IPOs in the week ahead. Construction equipment rental platform EquipmentShare.com (EQPT), fleet management software provider Motive Technologies (MTVE), fuel distributor ARKO Petroleum (APC), and Medtronic’s diabetes carve-out MiniMed (MMED) all could potentially launch after having submitted their initial filings at the end of December. Several other notable names that filed prior to December could also launch IPOs soon, including life insurance platform Ethos Technologies (LIFE), baby food producer Once Upon a Farm (OFRM), crypto investment platform Grayscale (GRAY), pan-Asian travel platform Klook (KLK), and cryptocurrency infrastructure provider BitGo (BTGO).

Street research is expected for two companies in the week ahead, and six lock-up periods will be expiring.

IPO Market Snapshot

The Renaissance IPO Indices are market cap weighted baskets of newly public companies. As of 1/8/2026, the Renaissance IPO Index was up 3.9% year-to-date, while the S&P 500 was up 1.1%. Renaissance Capital’s IPO ETF (IPO) tracks the index, and top ETF holdings include Reddit (RDDT) and Kenvue (KVUE). The Renaissance International IPO Index was up 7.2% year-to-date, while the ACWX was up 2.0%. Renaissance Capital’s International IPO ETF (IPOS) tracks the index, and top ETF holdings include Kioxia and Galderma.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Valens Semiconductor: Sell The 65% Pop, Fundamentals Are Flimsy
data.description:
Shares of semiconductor firm Valens Semiconductor (VLN) surged as much as 65% on Friday. There appeared to be no tangible news to justify the rally. Rather, it appears the stock popped thanks to a posting on X, the site formerly known as Twitter. A user there with tens of thousands of followers published an extensive bullish thesis on Valens, and numerous other users followed suit with their own upbeat takes on the company.

This social media activity seemingly helped jumpstart the 65% run in the stock, which came on far higher than usual volume:

Is Valens an unheralded gem? Or will this be a short-term pop that quickly fades? As the headline suggests, I don't think there is all that much interesting going on at Valens. You can count me in the bearish camp following Friday's run.

The Bullish Case

The post I alluded to above makes two major arguments.

The first of these is that there was ticker confusion between Valens Semiconductor, which is traded as "VLN" on the NYSE and Velan Inc. (VLN:CA), which uses the same ticker symbol but trades in Canada.

Apparently, some online sites published articles about Valens, the U.S.-listed company, using financial data for the unrelated Canadian firm. This led to the mistaken conclusion that Valens (the semiconductor company) had too much inventory on its balance sheet and was likely to take a loss on that going forward. A bull could argue that the market has temporarily depressed the value of Valens Semiconductor stock due to this mistake around the company's inventory level and perceived likelihood of writedowns in 2026.

That analysis is correct up to this point. Valens Semiconductor had just $11.0 million in inventories as of last quarter, and there's no reason to expect a significant write-down on that modest sum anytime soon. I do wonder, though, how much the market overall really cared about some errant online financial reporting around inventory figures. It's possible that this led to some selling in Valens' shares, but I doubt it was an overwhelming factor in the company's underperformance over the past few years.

The other main point made is that Valens will be able to greatly widen its client base due to repositioning its semiconductor offerings for a broader range of customers and industries. The bullish takeaway here would be that the company has a path to stronger revenue growth, and it could also see rising profit margins. To back up this point, bulls can point to the company's recent Consumer Electronics Show presentation, where it unveiled more advanced e-mirror technology.

Throwing Some Cold Water On The Upside Case

I'll fully agree with the premise that Valens Semiconductor has a solid balance sheet.

The company had $93.5 million of cash and short-term investments as of last quarter. On the liability side of things, the company has no long-term debt and just $27.9 million of total liabilities. That's a long runway for operations. There are no apparent issues with the balance sheet, either in terms of inventories or with debt and other liabilities.

Some people mentioned the warrants as a concern, due to dilution. It's true that Valens was a former SPAC and still has warrants outstanding. But, like many SPACs, it plunged sharply out of the gate and never recovered; the $11.50 strike SPAC warrants remain way out of the money now:

If the stock actually gets back to the double digits and causes the warrants to go into the money, I doubt anyone buying at recent prices would care too much. And, as we'll see in a moment, Valens' actual operating business is rather uninspiring. In my opinion, it would be a blessing for the company if Valens could convert those warrants into cash at $11.50 per share.

Speaking about the business, like many former SPACs, Valens has struggled to sustain much operational momentum since going public:

Revenues peaked in 2023 and have fallen sharply since then. Bulls can now point to double-digit revenue growth in recent quarters, but that's coming off a low base.

The fundamental issue here is that Valens is selling a niche semiconductor product primarily into a market (autos) that is not particularly in favor right now. High interest rates and a restrained consumer spending environment have caused a downturn in the car market as compared to the boom days back in 2020-22.

People may associate semiconductors with AI, data centers, and other glamorous verticals. But many semi firms serving traditional industrial clients have actually faced a soft demand environment over the past few years, and Valens appears to be in that camp. We can't just assume that because this is a semiconductor firm, it will benefit from the same tailwinds that have helped AI-related companies.

The broader concern is that there's not much tangible evidence that Valens will ever become a meaningfully profitable business.

Consider the following chart. Since its SPAC deal concluded, Valens has spent, on virtually a one-to-one basis, every dollar of gross profit that comes in on R&D:

Needless to say, you're going to struggle to generate shareholder value if your R&D budget alone consumes the entirety of the profit you make in selling your wares.

Given that there are other costs, such as SG&A, associated with running a business, Valens is generating a large all-in loss. It has run at least a $20 million operating loss each year since it began filing financial information, and this loss ballooned to $40.6 million in 2024.

While the company's balance sheet is fine now and there may be market confusion around its inventory position, the real thesis-killer is much more simple. This is a sub-scale business serving a niche need within a mature cyclical industry. Automotive semis are not a hot market segment right now, and there's little evidence that Valens can become a secular growth story rather than a cyclical operation in the near term.

Bulls can point to potential expansion to markets like robotics. If that actually happens, wonderful, we can reassess the company at that point. But as long as this is a small operation with an up-and-down outlook selling primarily to a handful of automotive companies, the market is not going to assign much of a multiple to the operating business.

The current analyst consensus sees Valens losing 31 cents per share for 2025, losing 32 cents in 2026, and losing another 31 cents in 2027. Long story short, there's no imminent business inflection or turning point here. Valens is running wide operating losses, and it's likely to keep doing so for years to come.

Valens Semiconductor Bottom Line

The best thing I can say about this company is that it has a strong balance sheet. There's $1.01/share here in tangible book value as of last quarter. The cash and short-term investment pile should be enough to sustain the company for about three years at its current rate of losses. That's enough time for Valens to potentially either find new automotive clients or pivot into other industries that could have more sustainable growth prospects. And, if I were management, I'd look into issuing stock on this pop and fortify the balance sheet even further.

The question is, however, what value is there in Valens beyond its cash pile. Specifically, what is the operating part of the company worth? Keep in mind that it is losing about $30 million a year on $70 million of revenues, and that revenues peaked way back in 2023. Furthermore, automotive semis are far from the hottest category within the chips space at the moment.

I'd argue the operating business, in its current form, is not worth very much given the large amounts of red ink and unfavorable revenue trajectory. There is some optionality around the company's R&D budget and the potential for it to move into new or more attractive markets. However, I think the market has gotten far ahead of itself following Friday's rally. Unless the company finds new clients and greatly improves its profitability profile, I expect VLN stock to be trading back under $2 per share within a matter of weeks.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: MSDL And BXSL: High Yields Won't Save You From Negative Total Returns
data.description:
Investment thesis

The initial purpose of my article on the assets of the BDC Morgan Stanley Direct Lending Fund (MSDL) and Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL) is to make a direct comparison to determine whether either of them is worthy of inclusion in my portfolio. The final results of the fundamental analysis, though, show some negative stuff in how their risk-reward balance might change going forward. The current cycle of Fed rate cuts is hurting those BDC companies with the highest share of floating interest rates. And MSDL and BXSL have it at almost 100%. Given the current discount on the P/NAV metric, the value of their shares is trading 22% and 6% lower. At this time, I do not recommend buying these assets, expecting a continuation of the negative dynamics in their Total Return indicators.

Fundamental similarities and differences between MSDL and BXSL

Despite the fact that MSDL and BXSL have different management scales, there is an interesting dilemma between the "gold standard" of the BDC segment (in terms of portfolio risk assessment) and potentially undervalued assets (after all, both are trading at a discount to NAV). A number of qualities related to investment strategy and management structure make them similar:

  • Both companies are backed by global financial players (Morgan Stanley and Blackstone), both of which provide access to priority deals in the BDC category. These factors reduce the cost of customer acquisition and servicing. In addition, both companies are able to lend to large middle-market businesses, with special SEC approval allowing them to participate in co-investment programs in large credit lines involving their parent companies.
  • And since the parent companies are focused on credit portfolio security, MSDL and BXSL have adopted this strategy. Both adhere to a defensive strategy, granting loans to the most secure entities capable of securing loans with first lien rights. This means that in the event of a potential borrower default, the BDCs are the first to claim compensation from the assets sold.
  • These companies' credit policies are geared toward ensuring that the maximum share of their portfolios consists of floating interest rates. It means that their net income is correlated with the SOFR rate, currently at 3.66%. During periods of tightening monetary policy by the Fed, such a feature allowed them to protect themselves from rising costs. But during periods of Fed rate cuts, it is the main risk for reducing BDC margins.
  • Their high-quality management keeps the share of non-performing loans in their portfolios extremely low. It reflects their discipline in risk assessment. Also, they are known for their low base fees (at 1.0%). It makes them focused on scaling their operations rather than squeezing profits out of investors' assets.

In spite of these similarities, there are a number of differences between MSDL and BXSL that are important to mention. They can be divided into three groups: asset quality, operating efficiency with dividends, and borrowing costs.

Asset quality.

The quality of assets affects the level of credit risk. Neither company is particularly risky in this regard, though there is a difference in their more specialized customer focus. The MSDL prefers to work with a slightly smaller segment of small businesses than the BXSL. As a result, it currently has a non-accruals ratio of 1.2%. That's still a great number, though investors are more nervous about the decline in net asset value throughout 2025. At BSXL, the same non-accruals ratio is 0.1%. This is the benchmark for stability in credit risk assessment. The BXSL specializes in working with large medium-sized businesses with a minimum annual EBITDA of $100 million. As a result, the BXSL's borrowers are more economically mature and therefore more financially reliable. If there is an economic storm or systemic crisis in the economy, BXSL's portfolio will be more resilient than MSDL's.

Operational efficiency with dividends.

Differences in operational efficiency affect companies' dividend policy prospects. This is because the amount of net investment income per share determines the level of regular dividend coverage. For MSDL, it is close to 100%, whereas BXSL has a small margin of safety at 106% (data for Q3 2025). But it's going down. In Q3 2024, for example, the figure was 118%, and a year earlier it was 123%. This is a negative trend and signals a potential reduction in dividends in the future. Should MSDL's operational efficiency decline, the dividend coverage ratio will fall below the required level of 100%. After that, the question of reducing the dividends themselves will become relevant. In my opinion, that is the main reason why MSDL shares are trading at such a high discount to the P/NAV metric (0.78x).

Cost of borrowing.

MSDL and BXSL have roughly equal debt-to-equity (leverage) ratios (1.17x and 1.15x). In comparison with the median for the BDC sector, this is a healthy level that does not signal any danger for the company. Analyzing BXSL, note that this company aims to scale its loan portfolio through cheaper access to the bond market. That's a good thing for margin growth. At MSDL, they're actively working to optimize their current borrowing terms. In fact, just recently, the company closed its CLO issue with a rate of SOFR + 1.70%.

I would now like to draw your attention to the following table, where a comparison of a number of key indicators and metrics is presented.

Criterion

MSDL

BXSL

$13.8 billion, 311 portfolio companies

-3.56%

10.14%

The main metric in assessing the appeal of BDC assets for me is the Total Return indicator. Let's take a look at its value over the past year. Both companies are unprofitable. They differ only in that BXSL's loss is 2.46% less. Take a look at the two-year period (with the only adjustment being that it starts on January 23, 2024, because BXSL starts on that day). Here, we see different results, because MSDL remains unprofitable, whereas BXSL has a profit of 10.14%. I still think this is risky, since the Fed is going to keep cutting rates. And that's going to hurt net income, making my recommendation for these assets "Sell."

MSDL and BXSL valuation

In order to assess the attractiveness of MSDL and BXSL, let's first look at the P/NAV metric. This is negative for both companies. The biggest drop is seen in MSDL, with its shares trading at a discount of 0.78x, whereas BXSL is trading at 0.94x. There are 3 main reasons why MSDL shares have such a huge discount, whereas BXSL is closer to its par value:

  • Since the net asset value of MSDL is declining, markets are assessing the additional risks associated with the impact of floating rates. Around 99.6% of all loans are issued at floating rates, meaning that the net asset value will continue to decline. Given that there are no reserves for dividend payments, in my opinion, shareholder payouts are likely to be reduced.
  • The MSDL is not a long-standing player in the BDC sector and has not yet established itself as a "blue chip." No situation has yet arisen that would allow the company's portfolio to be truly tested for stress resistance. Since the economic situation of BDC began to deteriorate in 2025, the focus of investors is on selling less reliable assets first.
  • The size of MSDL's portfolio is $3.8 billion, with a market capitalization of $1.4 billion, lagging behind the major players in BDC. This asset therefore has lower liquidity, meaning it attracts less investment from various institutional investors.

Risks associated with Fed rate cuts

Floating rate loans account for 99.6% of MSDL's portfolio and 98.6% of BXSL's portfolio. This high proportion of floating rates is particularly unfortunate given that the current cycle of Fed rate cuts is likely to continue. The next Fed rate cut is already forecast for the meeting on April 29, 2026. Currently, there is a 54.0% probability of a 25 basis point cut (to 3.50%). Each time the Fed cuts rates, SOFR will also fall. That increases interest rate risk for companies such as MSDL and BXSL. That's why the main risk for these assets is the ability to maintain their previous margins. Lower margins make it difficult to pay the same dividends as before. Therefore, a reduction in regular payments could increase investor concerns about MSDL and BXSL, as a result of which the further dynamics of their share prices will continue to move within the previous bearish trend. In 2025, a high dividend yield of around 12% per annum was only able to offset half of the loss in share value. This is why the overall return was negative (-13.28% and -10.82%).

Conclusion

In this way, both MSDL and BXSL are quite similar, placing them in the same category of BDC companies. Both are focused on minimizing capital loss risk and pay high dividends (around 12% per annum). However, they have one of the lowest base fees (1.0%), while access to parent company borrowers increases their scaling potential. It is only the key metrics, like P/NAV and Regular Dividend Coverage, show a big difference, so the market and the huge discount to shares think MSDL is one of the BDC "outsiders." As a conservative investor, you should probably go with BXSL. But personally, I’d recommend "Sell". If the Fed continues its policy of easing conditions in the money market, I don't believe this interest rate-sensitive asset class will be able to maintain its previous payouts. Instead, choose other BDC assets that are capable of gaining additional dividends from the new cycle of rate cuts (for example, Hercules Capital (HTGC) and Ares Capital (ARCC)).

data.title: Phathom: Why I'm Not Buying The Dip (Yet)
data.description:
Introduction

Despite some lingering reservations, I have become less cautious of Phathom Pharmaceuticals (PHAT) over time.

My last analysis in September 2025 followed the achievement of VOQUEZNA’s NCE exclusivity extension into 2032, which was a critical development for the company.

Moreover, Phathom has optimized its sales efforts, focusing less on costly and low ROI direct-to-consumer ads and more on prescriber outreach, driving operating leverage.

Still, while I acknowledged that VOQUEZNA plays a clear role in severe erosive GERD, Phathom’s balance sheet (which includes debt obligations, revenue liability principal, and so on) means that “$1 of VOQUEZNA net revenue ≠ $1 of cash.” Moreover, I asked, “Can the company justify its valuation with just six or seven years of VOQUEZNA? And at what expense will this come to its shareholders if they don’t achieve profitability in 2026 as guided?”

I concluded that PHAT was a “mixed bag” and assigned a Hold rating.

On Thursday, the company released preliminary Q4 results and announced its intention to raise $130 million via a public offering.

The article that follows revisits its stock in light of recent developments.

Recent Developments

To say it upfront, Phathom is still less “near-term IP cliff” and more “can it convert a finite window into durable cash flow despite leverage and GTN?”

Its preliminary Q4 results and public offering support this assertion.

Q4 net revenue is estimated as ~$57-$58 million. Non-GAAP operating expenses were ~$51-$53 million with ~$6 million net cash usage. The company ended 2025 with ~$130 million in cash.

Recall that Phathom originally guided operating profitability for 2026. This was tightened to H2 2026 (excluding stock comp).

The equity financing priced $130 million gross at $16.00 per share. While this reduces near-term solvency risk, it also reinforces that Phathom’s balance sheet remains a gating factor.

The market did not respond kindly to these developments. As I am typing these words, PHAT is off ~13% in intraday trading.

Reading between the lines, the Q4 result implies that VOQUEZNA’s revenue ramp is still steep. Q3 net revenue was $49.5 million, so the Q4 prerelease implies a sequential step-up of roughly $7.5-$8.5 million (~15-17% Q/Q). This does weaken the “Phathom cannot execute the launch fast enough” bear thesis.

Similarly, “1 million dispensed prescriptions” for VOQUEZNA products implies that ~210k additional dispensed scripts after 10/17/25. Recall that in July, gastroenterologists (the specialists who manage severe erosive disease) accounted for ~70% of filled VOQUEZNA scripts to date, which supports that growth is occurring in the segment where VOQUEZNA’s differentiation is most defensible (erosive disease/severe GERD), as opposed to broad first-line replacement of PPIs, where GTN is theoretically much lower.

Circling back to costs, the cost reset looks real on a run-rate basis. Recall that Q2 non-GAAP opex were $86.1 million. The Q4 prerelease implies $51-$53 million non-GAAP opex, which falls below its earlier restructuring target of sub-$55 million exiting 2025. So, the 2026 “profitability” debate is now less about SG&A bloat and more about GTN stability and fixed charges outside opex lines.

Finally, the equity raise, which will confer roughly 11% dilution, reflects the reality that “operating profitability” is not the same as being free cash flow positive once royalties, interest, and other obligations are accounted for. This helps balance Phathom’s outlook in the opposite direction in that it validates that VOQUEZNA’s commercial gains are muddied by deep and ongoing obligations.

Valuation

As for inputs, Year 1 Revenue of $316.41 million is the consensus analyst estimate for 2026. Revenue Growth and FCF Margin of 15% and 20%, respectively, are my base estimates. The FCF Margin is an “assumption within an assumption” because we are also assuming that Phathom will be able to generate cash (this is not yet the case). Remember, both values are averages.

Most everything else was gathered from Phathom’s financial statements (e.g., Interest-Bearing Debt, Cost of Debt estimate, Cash & Equivalents) or is standard DCF methodology (e.g., Tax Rate, Terminal Growth).

Two things catch my attention here. For starters, even when I plug in optimistic assumptions (15% CAGR and 20% FCF margin), PHAT is overvalued by ~26%. Second, as is typical in DCF models, PHAT’s valuation depends a lot on the terminal value (cash flows after Year 9). So, a key question that should sit atop investors' minds is, “What does Phathom look like after VOQUEZNA’s NCE exclusivity expires in 2032?” Note that 2032 coincides with Year 7 in my model. In pharma, terminal value is very dependent on R&D innovation, which requires R&D investments. However, due to ongoing losses and debt/RIFI obligations, the company is restricted there, and its pipeline is tied to just one drug with limited label expansion opportunities (e.g., as-needed dosing for “heartburn” and treatment of eosinophilic esophagitis).

Upon performing a Sensitivity Analysis, PHAT appears priced for 18.7% CAGR (assuming 20% FCF margin). Similarly, if FCF margin bumps to 24.6% (with CAGR remaining at 15%), PHAT becomes appropriately valued.

As you may already be aware, judging by PHAT’s 52-week range ($2.21-$18.31), it is ultra-sensitive to assumptions.

My Monte Carlo simulation shows how even small changes to key assumptions impact valuation.

For now, pending fundamental changes that would materially impact assumptions, PHAT’s “floor” and “ceiling” prices can be interpreted as $7.67 and $24.76. Of course, this is a wide range, consistent with the uncertainty involved with PHAT.

Final Words

Net-net, I remain at Hold.

While VOQUEZNA is a drug to be taken seriously, Phathom is tasked with driving volume and resetting costs. The Q4 prerelease signals that both are moving in the right direction.

On the other hand, the equity raise is a reminder that the balance sheet remains a gating factor. Operating profitability in H2 2026 is a nice goal to have, but don’t confuse it yet with free cash flow once interest, royalties, and the RIFI structure are accounted for.

On valuation, my base case requires continued execution and does not leave a lot of room for error. After all, PHAT is up >100% in the past year, so it would not surprise me to see it cool off here.

Until there is clearer evidence of durable GTN, a steadier path to free cash flow, reducing financing overhang, and better long-term prospects (that support terminal value assumptions), I’m happy to remain on the sidelines.
